messages.content: You are a practical, friendly assistant for everyday how-to advice.
I’ve never changed a flat tire before. Can you walk me through it so I can do it safely on my own?

Absolutely. Read your vehicle’s manual for specifics, then follow these general steps:

Gear you’ll need
- Spare tire (inflated), jack, lug wrench, wheel chocks (or heavy blocks), gloves, and the owner’s manual. A flashlight is handy at night.

1) Get to a safe spot
- Pull off the road to a level shoulder or parking lot. Turn on hazard lights. Set the parking brake.

2) Secure the vehicle
- Place wheel chocks/blocks on the opposite side of the flat (in front of and behind the tire) to prevent rolling.

3) Prep the wheel
- Remove hubcap/wheel cover if needed.
- Loosen lug nuts one turn while the tire is still on the ground (lefty-loosey). Use body weight carefully—don’t jump on the wrench.

4) Position the jack
- Locate the correct jacking point (see manual). Place the jack on firm, level ground and raise the vehicle until the flat is just off the ground.

5) Remove the flat
- Fully remove lug nuts and keep them in a safe spot. Pull the wheel straight off the hub.

6) Mount the spare
- Align the spare with the studs and push it on. Hand-tighten lug nuts in a star pattern.

7) Lower and tighten
- Lower the vehicle until the tire just contacts the ground. Tighten lug nuts firmly in a star pattern.
- Fully lower the car and do a final tighten in the same pattern. If you have a torque spec, use a torque wrench to finish.

8) Stow and check
- Put the flat, jack, and tools away. Ensure the spare’s pressure is adequate (check the sidewall for PSI). Reinstall the hubcap if it fits the spare.

9) Drive with care
- Temporary “donut” spares are for short distances at reduced speeds—get the original tire repaired or replaced as soon as possible.

Tips
- If the ground is soft, a flat board under the jack increases stability.
- If a lug nut is stuck, a penetrating oil and a few minutes of wait time can help.
- Never put any part of your body under the vehicle when supported only by the jack.
